title: Org-Scoped Platform Admin Tenancy
context: Platform administrators need to manage configuration across multiple organisations. Two models were evaluated:

**Model A (cross-org views):** Platform admins see all organisations simultaneously. Requires bypassing RLS, conditional `if (isPlatformAdmin)` branches in every query, and a separate code path for admin vs regular users.

**Model B (org-scoped):** Platform admins are always scoped to one organisation at a time via JWT `tenant_id`. They switch context via the tenant switcher, which issues a new JWT. Same code path as regular users.
decision: **Model B — org-scoped platform admin tenancy.** Platform admins switch organisations via token reissuance.
- JWT always contains `tenant_id` — no null-tenant code paths
- RLS applies identically to platform admins and regular users
- Tenant switching returns a new JWT (page reload or re-authentication)
- One code path for all authorization levels — no `if (isPlatformAdmin)` in queries
**`EnablePlatformAdminBypass()`** is available for the narrow set of endpoints that genuinely need cross-org visibility (e.g., organisation list page). New uses require explicit architectural review.
consequences: - One code path — eliminates conditional branching across the entire codebase
- RLS integrity preserved structurally, not by convention
- No cross-org data leaks via forgotten `isPlatformAdmin` checks
- Audit trail is always tenant-scoped — clear accountability
- Platform admins cannot compare two organisations side-by-side without switching (UX friction)
- New organisation creation must handle platform admin membership provisioning
- Auto-enrollment of platform admins when new organisations are created
- Multi-tab workflow support (each tab may need its own JWT context)
- Governance for `EnablePlatformAdminBypass()` — maintain an explicit allow-list
origin: Identity convention (Identity ADR-0021), adopted by Actions for one-code-path simplification.
